query_text: The starter engages but won't turn the engine -- I hear a grinding noise and the starter just spins.
validated_fix: Inspect the starter adapter and flywheel ring gear. The Lycoming O-320 uses a starter adapter assembly that engages the ring gear. Common failures: (1) Worn or damaged starter adapter clutch -- the adapter contains an overrunning clutch that can wear, preventing proper engagement. Replace the starter adapter (Lycoming P/N LW-16471 or current replacement). (2) Damaged ring gear teeth -- if teeth are chipped or worn, the starter can't engage. Replace the ring gear (requires engine removal). (3) Worn starter drive gear (Bendix drive). Replace the starter or starter drive. NOTE: A grinding starter that doesn't turn the engine is NOT a minor issue -- continued attempts can further damage the ring gear and adapter. Diagnose before attempting more starts.

source_citations.source: 1975_cessna_172_skyhawk_aviation/.splits_Cessna 172 & Skyhawk Series Service Manual (1969 - 1976)/Cessna 172 & Skyhawk Series Service Manual (1969 - 1976)_part04_p151-200.pdf
source_citations.context: 11- 69. TROUBLE SHOOTING > TROUBLE > STARTER MOTOR RUNS, BUT DOES NOT TURN CRANKSHAFT.

source_citations.source: 1975_cessna_172_skyhawk_aviation/.splits_Cessna 172 & Skyhawk Series Service Manual (1969 - 1976)/Cessna 172 & Skyhawk Series Service Manual (1969 - 1976)_part04_p151-200.pdf
source_citations.context: TROUBLE > PROBABLE CAUSE > STARTER MOTOR RUNS, BUT

response_parsed.diagnostic_reasoning: The symptom 'starter engages but won't turn the engine' combined with 'grinding noise and the starter just spins' points directly to a failure in the mechanical power transfer from the starter motor to the engine's crankshaft. The troubleshooting guide (11-69, 11A-67) lists 'Defective Bendix drive,' 'Damaged starter pinion gear or ring gear,' and 'Defective overrunning clutch or drive' as probable causes when the 'STARTER MOTOR RUNS, BUT DOES NOT TURN CRANKSHAFT.' The 'STARTER EXCESSIVELY NOISY' symptom further supports 'Worn starter pinion gear or broken teeth on ring gear.' The Bendix drive (or overrunning clutch) is responsible for engaging the starter pinion gear with the engine's ring gear. If either the Bendix drive fails to fully engage, or if the teeth on either gear are worn or broken, the starter motor will spin, but the engine will not turn, and a grinding noise will be present.
response_parsed.mechanic_instructions: The grinding noise and the starter spinning without turning the crankshaft indicate a mechanical failure in the starter engagement mechanism. We need to determine if the issue is with the starter's Bendix drive (overrunning clutch) or the gears themselves. You need to physically inspect the starter pinion gear and the engine's ring gear for damage, and verify the Bendix drive's operation.
answer_path_prompts: I removed the starter and found the starter pinion gear teeth are worn or broken.
I removed the starter and found the engine's ring gear teeth are worn or broken.
I removed the starter and the pinion gear and ring gear appear fine, but the Bendix drive (overrunning clutch) does not extend or retract properly, or it slips when manually tested.

answer_paths: I removed the starter and found the starter pinion gear teeth are worn or broken.
I removed the starter and found the engine's ring gear teeth are worn or broken.
I removed the starter and the pinion gear and ring gear appear fine, but the Bendix drive (overrunning clutch) does not extend or retract properly, or it slips when manually tested.
mechanic_instructions: The grinding noise and the starter spinning without turning the crankshaft indicate a mechanical failure in the starter engagement mechanism. We need to determine if the issue is with the starter's Bendix drive (overrunning clutch) or the gears themselves. You need to physically inspect the starter pinion gear and the engine's ring gear for damage, and verify the Bendix drive's operation.
diagnostic_reasoning: The symptom 'starter engages but won't turn the engine' combined with 'grinding noise and the starter just spins' points directly to a failure in the mechanical power transfer from the starter motor to the engine's crankshaft. The troubleshooting guide (11-69, 11A-67) lists 'Defective Bendix drive,' 'Damaged starter pinion gear or ring gear,' and 'Defective overrunning clutch or drive' as probable causes when the 'STARTER MOTOR RUNS, BUT DOES NOT TURN CRANKSHAFT.' The 'STARTER EXCESSIVELY NOISY' symptom further supports 'Worn starter pinion gear or broken teeth on ring gear.' The Bendix drive (or overrunning clutch) is responsible for engaging the starter pinion gear with the engine's ring gear. If either the Bendix drive fails to fully engage, or if the teeth on either gear are worn or broken, the starter motor will spin, but the engine will not turn, and a grinding noise will be present.

query_text: The trim wheel is stiff and the trim doesn't respond smoothly -- it's hard to get the airplane properly trimmed.
validated_fix: Inspect and service/replace the elevator trim system. The 172 uses a cable-actuated trim tab on the elevator. Common issues: (1) Trim cable wear/corrosion -- replace the trim cable if frayed or corroded. (2) Trim cable pulleys -- inspect for wear, flat spots, and bearing failure. Replace as needed. (3) Trim tab actuator -- the jackscrew mechanism on the tab can become dry or corroded. Lubricate with approved lubricant. (4) Trim wheel mechanism -- the drum and cable can become stiff from dirt/corrosion inside the cabin mechanism. Clean and lubricate. After any trim system work, verify full travel and correct sense (wheel forward = nose down, wheel aft = nose up). Check the trim tab for security and free play within limits. NOTE: An inoperative or jammed trim system affects controllability -- per the POH, the airplane can be flown without trim, but pilot workload increases significantly, especially on long flights.

response_parsed.diagnostic_reasoning: The symptom 'stiff trim wheel' and 'doesn't respond smoothly' indicates excessive resistance within the elevator trim control system. The Service Manual's troubleshooting section for 'TRIM CONTROL WHEEL MOVES WITH EXCESSIVE RESISTANCE' lists several probable causes, including high cable tension, binding pulleys, rusty chains, damaged sprockets, bent sprocket shafts, trim tab hinge binding, and a defective trim tab actuator (Source 1, 15, 41, 50). The 'doesn't respond smoothly' aspect further points to friction or intermittent binding in these mechanical components. The initial diagnostic steps should involve visual inspection of the control system components and isolating the trim tab and actuator to determine the source of resistance.
response_parsed.mechanic_instructions: The trim wheel's stiffness and unsmooth response point to friction or binding in the elevator trim control system. We need to isolate the source of this resistance. First, we'll check the control cables, pulleys, chains, and sprockets. Then, we'll check the trim tab hinge and actuator.
answer_path_prompts: I have opened the access plates and visually inspected the trim system cables, pulleys, chains, and sprockets. I observe rust, damage, or cables off pulleys, or clear signs of binding.

I have opened the access plates and visually inspected the trim system cables, pulleys, chains, and sprockets. All components appear to be in good condition, properly routed, and free of obvious damage or rust.
I have disconnected the trim tab actuator from the trim tab horn assembly. The trim tab moves freely by hand with no resistance.
I have disconnected the trim tab actuator from the trim tab horn assembly. The trim tab still exhibits resistance when moved by hand.

query_text: The carb heat knob is very stiff to pull and doesn't seem to give full heat -- or it broke off in my hand.
technical_issue: Carburetor Heat Control Cable Stiff / Broken
validated_fix: Replace or service the carburetor heat control cable. The carb heat cable operates a valve on the carburetor air box that diverts intake air through a shroud around the exhaust to pre-heat it. If the cable is stiff: lubricate with cable lubricant and check for kinks in the routing. If the cable is broken or permanently stuck: replace the cable (Cessna P/N per the IPC for your specific model). After replacement, verify full travel -- the valve must open completely for full carb heat and close completely for cold air. CRITICAL: Carburetor heat is a REQUIRED safety system. An inoperative carb heat cable is a grounding item -- the aircraft cannot be dispatched until it's repaired. Carburetor ice can cause complete engine failure, and carb heat is the only means of preventing and removing it on the O-320-E2D installation.

response_parsed.diagnostic_reasoning: The carburetor heat control is a push-pull type that operates a valve in the induction airbox. Stiffness or breakage of the knob indicates a mechanical binding or failure within this system. Potential causes include: 1) binding within the control cable or its housing, 2) a sticking or obstructed air valve in the induction airbox, or 3) damage to the control wire or its attachment to the air valve lever. The most efficient first step is to isolate the control cable from the airbox valve to determine which component is causing the resistance.
response_parsed.mechanic_instructions:
The carburetor heat control is a push-pull type that operates a valve in the induction airbox. Stiffness or breakage indicates a mechanical issue within this system. To isolate the problem, you need to determine if the binding is in the control cable assembly or the airbox valve itself.

First, remove the engine cowling as per paragraph 11-3. Then, locate the carburetor heat control connection at the airbox. Disconnect the control wire from the air valve lever on the airbox. This will allow you to test the movement of the control knob and the air valve independently. Observe the movement of both components.
answer_path_prompts: The control knob now moves freely, but the air valve on the airbox is stiff or stuck.
The control knob is still stiff or broken, even after disconnecting it from the airbox valve.
Both the control knob and the air valve move freely after disconnection.

query_text: The engine runs rough and the mechanic found that the magneto timing has drifted from the specification.
validated_fix: Retime the magnetos. The O-320-E2D specification is 20° BTDC (both magnetos). Use a timing light/buzzer (Eastern Electronics E-50 or equivalent) to verify and set the timing. Set the crankshaft to the correct position using the timing marks on the flywheel/ring gear. Adjust the magneto body position (internal point gap affects timing -- points must be gapped per the mag manufacturer before timing). Verify both mags fire at 20° BTDC ± 1°. NOTE: Magneto timing can drift due to: (1) Point wear changing the point gap over time. (2) Magneto mounting hardware loosening. (3) Impulse coupling wear. Timing should be verified at every annual/100-hour inspection and after any magneto service.

response_parsed.diagnostic_reasoning: The reported symptom of 'engine runs rough' combined with the observation that 'magneto timing has drifted from the specification' points directly to the ignition system. The primary suspect is incorrect magneto-to-engine timing. The Service Manual specifies checking magneto-to-engine timing at regular intervals (e.g., every 100 or 200 hours depending on engine type) and provides a tolerance of +0, -2 degrees (Source 5, 9, 10, 26). If the timing is outside this range, it must be re-timed to the engine (Source 9, 26). If re-timing is not possible or the timing drifts immediately, it suggests an internal magneto defect, especially for Lycoming 'Blue Streak' magnetos which are not field overhaulable and have non-replaceable internal components (Source 2, 15, 18, 34). Other potential causes for rough running, such as fouled spark plugs or a defective ignition harness, should also be considered if timing adjustment does not resolve the issue (Source 9, 23, 33, 40, 41).
response_parsed.mechanic_instructions: The reported drift in magneto timing is a critical issue for engine operation. Your next step is to perform a magneto-to-engine timing check and adjustment. This procedure involves setting the number one cylinder to its advanced firing position and then using a timing light to ensure the magneto's contact points break at the correct position. The specified tolerance for magneto-to-engine timing is plus zero, minus two degrees. Refer to paragraph 11-57 (or 11A-55 for Continental engines) for detailed instructions on locating the advanced firing position and paragraph 11-58 (or 11A-56) for the timing check and adjustment procedure. (Source 5, 9, 18, 26, 27, 28, 39, 48, 50).
answer_path_prompts: I measured the magneto-to-engine timing, and it was outside the +0, -2 degree tolerance, but I successfully adjusted it back into specification. The engine now runs smoothly.

I measured the magneto-to-engine timing, and it was outside the +0, -2 degree tolerance. I attempted to adjust it, but it either could not be brought into specification or immediately drifted out again.
I measured the magneto-to-engine timing, and it was within the +0, -2 degree tolerance. The engine still runs rough.

answer_paths: I measured the magneto-to-engine timing, and it was outside the +0, -2 degree tolerance, but I successfully adjusted it back into specification. The engine now runs smoothly.

I measured the magneto-to-engine timing, and it was outside the +0, -2 degree tolerance. I attempted to adjust it, but it either could not be brought into specification or immediately drifted out again.
I measured the magneto-to-engine timing, and it was within the +0, -2 degree tolerance. The engine still runs rough.
mechanic_instructions: The reported drift in magneto timing is a critical issue for engine operation. Your next step is to perform a magneto-to-engine timing check and adjustment. This procedure involves setting the number one cylinder to its advanced firing position and then using a timing light to ensure the magneto's contact points break at the correct position. The specified tolerance for magneto-to-engine timing is plus zero, minus two degrees. Refer to paragraph 11-57 (or 11A-55 for Continental engines) for detailed instructions on locating the advanced firing position and paragraph 11-58 (or 11A-56) for the timing check and adjustment procedure. (Source 5, 9, 18, 26, 27, 28, 39, 48, 50).
diagnostic_reasoning: The reported symptom of 'engine runs rough' combined with the observation that 'magneto timing has drifted from the specification' points directly to the ignition system. The primary suspect is incorrect magneto-to-engine timing. The Service Manual specifies checking magneto-to-engine timing at regular intervals (e.g., every 100 or 200 hours depending on engine type) and provides a tolerance of +0, -2 degrees (Source 5, 9, 10, 26). If the timing is outside this range, it must be re-timed to the engine (Source 9, 26). If re-timing is not possible or the timing drifts immediately, it suggests an internal magneto defect, especially for Lycoming 'Blue Streak' magnetos which are not field overhaulable and have non-replaceable internal components (Source 2, 15, 18, 34). Other potential causes for rough running, such as fouled spark plugs or a defective ignition harness, should also be considered if timing adjustment does not resolve the issue (Source 9, 23, 33, 40, 41).

query_text: My altimeter doesn't match the field elevation when I set the current altimeter setting -- it's off by over 75 feet.
validated_fix: Have the altimeter inspected and calibrated by a certified instrument repair station. Per FAR 91.411, the altimeter must be inspected every 24 calendar months for IFR operations. The inspection verifies accuracy at multiple pressure altitudes and checks for scale error, hysteresis, and friction error. Allowable tolerance: ±75 feet at sea level. If the altimeter is out of tolerance, it must be overhauled/calibrated or replaced. NOTE: Even for VFR flight, an inaccurate altimeter is a safety concern -- incorrect altitude indications can lead to CFIT (Controlled Flight Into Terrain) or airspace violations. Per FAR 91.205, an adjustable altimeter is required for all flight.

response_parsed.diagnostic_reasoning: The reported symptom of the altimeter being off by over 75 feet when set to field elevation falls under 'INCORRECT INDICATION' for the altimeter (Source 6, 15-20). The probable causes for this include a plugged static line, a defective altimeter mechanism, leaking diaphragm, pointers out of calibration, static pressure irregularities, or a leak in other static instrument installations (Source 6, 15-20). The static system is shared by the altimeter, vertical speed indicator, and airspeed indicator (Source 43, 15-11). Therefore, the primary differential diagnosis is between a fault within the altimeter itself or a problem with the shared static pressure system.
response_parsed.mechanic_instructions: Your altimeter is indicating incorrectly. This could be due to a fault within the altimeter instrument itself, or a problem with the shared static pressure system that supplies pressure to the altimeter, vertical speed indicator (VSI), and airspeed indicator. To narrow this down, observe the behavior of the other static instruments.
answer_path_prompts: The vertical speed indicator (VSI) and airspeed indicator also show incorrect or sluggish responses, or fail to operate correctly.
Only the altimeter shows an incorrect indication; the VSI and airspeed indicator appear to be operating normally.

query_text: The mechanic found corrosion at the wing strut attach fittings during the annual inspection.
validated_fix: Inspect and remediate per Cessna Service Manual and applicable inspection criteria. The wing strut attach fittings are critical structural components -- the struts carry a significant portion of the wing loads. Treatment: (1) Remove the bolt, clean and inspect the bolt hole for elongation or corrosion. (2) Inspect the fitting for corrosion depth -- if beyond allowable limits, the fitting must be replaced per approved data. (3) Clean corrosion with approved methods, treat with conversion coating, and apply protective primer. (4) Replace the bolt if it shows any corrosion, wear, or elongation. (5) Use approved corrosion inhibitor in the bolt hole before reassembly. NOTE: Water accumulates at strut fittings because they are at the low point of the wing contour. Drain holes must be clear. This is a recurring inspection item -- especially on aircraft operated in coastal or humid environments.

response_parsed.diagnostic_reasoning: Corrosion on wing strut attach fittings is a critical structural concern. The Service Manual states that wing strut repair is limited to replacement of tie-downs and attaching parts, and a badly dented, cracked, or deformed wing strut should be replaced (Section 4-11, page 17). General structural repair guidelines emphasize that 'corrosion of all types must be carefully considered, and approved remedial action taken' (Section 18-56, page 40). For specific aircraft serial numbers (17261664 THRU 17261808), wing strut attachment straps require inspection and compliance with Cessna Single-engine Service Letter SE73-20 (Note, page 18). The immediate next step is to determine the extent of the corrosion and check the aircraft's serial number against the service letter.
response_parsed.mechanic_instructions: Corrosion on wing strut attach fittings demands immediate attention due to its impact on structural integrity. We need to determine the severity of the corrosion and whether a specific service bulletin applies to this aircraft. Carefully inspect the affected area.
answer_path_prompts: The corrosion is superficial, easily removed, and has not caused any pitting, material loss, or visible cracks.
The corrosion has caused pitting, significant material loss, or there are visible cracks in the fitting.
The aircraft serial number is between 17261664 and 17261808.

query_text: I hear a whining noise in my radios that changes pitch with engine RPM -- the mechanic calls it alternator whine.
validated_fix: Diagnose and eliminate the RF interference: (1) Check the alternator filter (capacitor) -- replace if failed. (2) Check all avionics and instrument grounds -- a corroded ground connection allows noise to couple into the audio. Clean and tighten all ground connections. (3) Check the ignition harness shielding -- the 172 uses shielded ignition leads. If the shield grounding is broken or corroded, ignition noise bleeds into avionics. Verify all shield grounds are clean and tight. (4) Check for a faulty voltage regulator -- a noisy regulator injects ripple onto the bus. (5) Install a noise filter on the alternator output if not already present. NOTE: Avionics interference is almost always caused by a grounding problem. Systematic cleaning and verification of all avionics, engine, and airframe ground connections usually resolves the issue.

response_parsed.diagnostic_reasoning: The symptom of 'alternator whine' changing pitch with engine RPM strongly indicates an issue within the alternator power system. This noise is typically caused by AC ripple on the DC bus due to improper rectification, unstable voltage regulation, or inadequate noise suppression. The Service Manual explicitly states that 'poor voltage regulation and/or excessive radio noise may result' from issues with alternator grounding, wiring shields, or the regulator base (Source 53, page 8). Furthermore, troubleshooting for related electrical issues like 'Ammeter indicates heavy discharge' points to a 'Shorted radio noise filter or shorted wire' (Source 1, page 3; Source 13, page 3; Source 17, page 3) or 'Shorted diodes in alternator' (Source 5, page 4; Source 34, page 4), and a 'Regulator faulty or improperly adjusted' (Source 5, page 4; Source 9, page 4; Source 21, page 4; Source 34, page 4; Source 37, page 5; Source 46, page 4). These are the most probable causes for the reported symptom.
response_parsed.mechanic_instructions: The whining noise in your radios that changes pitch with engine RPM is a classic sign of electrical interference originating from the alternator system. This can be due to a faulty alternator, an improperly functioning voltage regulator, or issues with the wiring, grounding, or radio noise filter. We need to isolate the source of this electrical noise. Start by checking the components designed to suppress this noise and the primary sources of electrical generation.
answer_path_prompts: I found a direct short in the radio noise filter or its wiring, or the filter shows infinite resistance in both directions during the resistance check (indicating an open circuit).

I performed the resistance check on the alternator output terminal to the alternator case, and it shows continuity in both directions, or infinite resistance in both directions (indicating shorted or open diodes).
I observed the field relay in the voltage regulator, and it is inoperative, or after jumpering the 'A+' and 'F' terminals, the ammeter does not show a heavy charge rate.

Furthermore, troubleshooting for related electrical issues like 'Ammeter indicates heavy discharge' points to a 'Shorted radio noise filter or shorted wire' (Source 1, page 3; Source 13, page 3; Source 17, page 3) or 'Shorted diodes in alternator' (Source 5, page 4; Source 34, page 4), and a 'Regulator faulty or improperly adjusted' (Source 5, page 4; Source 9, page 4; Source 21, page 4; Source 34, page 4; Source 37, page 5; Source 46, page 4). These are the most probable causes for the reported symptom.

query_text: There's oil dripping from the oil cooler and the oil level keeps dropping.
validated_fix: Replace or repair the oil cooler. Remove the cooler, inspect for external leaks at fittings and at the core. If a fitting is leaking, replace the fitting and O-ring/gasket. If the core is leaking (common failure from corrosion, vibration fatigue, or thermal cycling), replace the oil cooler with a new FAA-PMA approved unit. Use new hoses and fittings as needed. After installation, check the oil level and run the engine to operating temperature, then check for leaks. NOTE: Oil on the exhaust system is a fire hazard. Any oil cooler leak must be repaired before further flight. Also verify the cooler vernatherm (bypass valve) is functioning -- if stuck open, the oil bypasses the cooler entirely, leading to high oil temperatures.

source_citations.context: 11-29. ENGINE OIL SYSTEM.

response_parsed.diagnostic_reasoning: The reported symptoms, 'oil dripping from the oil cooler' and 'oil level keeps dropping,' directly indicate a leak in the engine oil system, specifically at or around the oil cooler. The service manual lists 'Low oil supply' as a probable cause for low oil pressure, and remedies include checking the dipstick and filling the sump (Source 30, 32, 34, 44). The oil cooler is mounted on the firewall with flexible hoses carrying oil to and from it (Source 16). Leaks can occur from the cooler core, its hoses, or connections. Other potential leak points, such as the crankshaft seal or push rod housing oil seal, are also listed under 'OIL LEAK AT FRONT OF ENGINE' (Source 10, 19, 26), but the user's observation points directly to the cooler. The initial step is to visually inspect the oil cooler and its connections to pinpoint the exact source of the leak.
response_parsed.mechanic_instructions: The oil dripping from the oil cooler and the dropping oil level indicate a leak in the engine oil system. You need to visually inspect the oil cooler assembly and its connections to determine the exact source of the leak. Clean the area around the oil cooler and run the engine briefly to observe where the oil begins to seep or drip.
answer_path_prompts: Oil is visibly seeping or dripping directly from the fins or body of the oil cooler itself.
Oil is dripping from one of the flexible hoses connected to the oil cooler, or from the fittings where the hoses attach to the cooler or engine.
Oil appears to be dripping onto the oil cooler from a component located above it, indicating the cooler isn't the primary leak source.

query_text: The mechanic found a crack in the nose gear fork during the 100-hour inspection.
validated_fix: Replace the nose gear fork per AD 71-22-02. This AD applies to Cessna 150, 172, 175, and 182 series with earlier-type nose gear forks. The AD requires a dye-penetrant inspection every 100 hours (or after a hard landing or severe shimmy) for cracks in the radius of the milled section of the nose gear strut attachment bolt. If cracks are found, the fork must be replaced with the updated part number per the current Cessna Parts Catalog. If the airplane has 1,500+ hours total time, the earlier fork must be replaced regardless of crack findings. NOTE: This is a recurring AD -- compliance must be documented in the airframe logbook at every 100-hour/annual inspection. The nose gear fork is a critical structural component -- a failed fork results in nose gear collapse.

response_parsed.diagnostic_reasoning: A crack in the nose gear fork is a critical structural defect. The Service Manual states that 'damaged or questionable forgings must be replaced' (Source 5, page 41; Source 11, page 41). While this specific reference is for landing gear bulkheads, the principle applies to any structural forging like the nose gear fork. The troubleshooting section also indicates to 'replace defective parts' for landing gear issues (Source 13, page 24). The nose gear shock strut disassembly procedure details removing the fork (Source 8, page 49). Therefore, the cracked nose gear fork requires replacement.
response_parsed.mechanic_instructions: The cracked nose gear fork is a flight safety issue and requires immediate replacement. Do not attempt to repair it. You will need to remove the nose gear shock strut assembly from the aircraft, then disassemble the strut to replace the fork. Refer to paragraph 5-26 for Nose Gear Removal and Installation, and paragraph 5-33 for Nose Gear Shock Strut Disassembly. Specifically, step 'h' of paragraph 5-33 details removing the bolt securing the fork and lower strut.
answer_path_prompts: I have removed and replaced the nose gear fork and reassembled the strut.

query_text: The fuel selector is leaking internally -- fuel is cross-feeding between tanks or dripping from the valve.
validated_fix: Overhaul the fuel selector valve. Remove the valve and replace all internal O-rings and seals with fuel-resistant materials (Viton or equivalent). Note: some older O-rings were not compatible with modern avgas formulations and deteriorate rapidly. Reassemble, test for leaks, and verify all detent positions function correctly (LEFT, RIGHT, BOTH). Ensure the valve handle clearly indicates the selected position. NOTE: Any fuel leak inside the cabin is a critical safety issue. The selector valve is located under the floor in the cabin area -- leaked fuel creates vapor in the enclosed cockpit space, which is an explosion hazard.

source_citations.context: 2-44. FUEL SELECTOR VALVE

response_parsed.diagnostic_reasoning: The reported symptom of internal fuel selector valve leakage, specifically cross-feeding or dripping, points directly to a defective fuel selector valve. The Service Manual states that 'Valve repair consists of replacement of seals, springs, balls and other detail parts' (Source 1, 5), which are the components responsible for sealing and preventing cross-flow. The troubleshooting guide also lists 'Defective fuel selector valve' as a probable cause for fuel flow issues, with the remedy being 'Repair or replace selector valve' (Source 2, 13, 16). Other fuel system issues like plugged vents or strainers would typically manifest as fuel starvation or tank pressurization, not internal valve leakage or cross-feeding.
response_parsed.mechanic_instructions: Your report indicates a defective fuel selector valve. This is a critical component for fuel management and safety. The internal leakage, whether cross-feeding or dripping, suggests worn or damaged internal seals, springs, or balls within the valve body. Before proceeding with any repair, you must physically verify the nature and extent of the leak. This will help determine if a seal replacement kit is sufficient or if the entire valve assembly requires replacement. Access to the valve requires removal of the pedestal cover (Source 4, 8).
answer_path_prompts: Fuel is visibly dripping from the fuel selector valve body or its connections beneath the pedestal.
Fuel levels in the tanks equalize or change unexpectedly when the selector is in a single tank position (LEFT or RIGHT), indicating cross-feeding.
The fuel selector handle is stiff, loose, or does not positively detent into positions, but no visible fuel leak is present.
No visible external leak, and fuel levels remain stable in single tank positions, but there is a strong, persistent fuel odor in the cabin.

query_text: The elevator hinge bolts are worn and there's excessive play in the elevator control surface.
validated_fix: Replace the worn hinge bolts and bushings. Remove the elevator, inspect all hinge points for elongated bolt holes and worn bushings. Replace bolts and bushings to restore proper fit with minimal play. Reassemble and verify control surface free play is within Cessna-specified limits. Also check the elevator horn for cracks or wear. After reassembly, re-rig the elevator travel and verify correct deflection limits per the Service Manual: UP = 28° ± 1°, DOWN = 23° ± 1° (verify for your specific model). NOTE: Excessive control surface play reduces control effectiveness and can cause flutter at high speeds. Flutter is a destructive oscillation that can destroy the control surface and tail structure in seconds. Any abnormal vibration or buzzing from the tail in flight requires immediate speed reduction and landing.

response_parsed.diagnostic_reasoning: The reported worn elevator hinge bolts and excessive play in the elevator control surface directly indicate 'Defective elevator hinges' as a probable cause for 'BINDING OR JUMPY MOTION FELT IN MOVEMENT OF ELEVATORS' (Source 1, 7, 15). The prescribed remedy is to 'Replace hinges found defective' (Source 1, 7, 15). This is a confirmed component failure.
response_parsed.mechanic_instructions: The worn elevator hinge bolts are causing excessive play. You need to replace the defective elevator hinges. To do this, follow the elevator removal procedure in Section 8-5 of the Service Manual. Specifically, you will need to 'Remove bolts (11) from elevator hinges' (Source 11, page 30) and then remove the elevator. After replacement, ensure you rig the system in accordance with paragraph 8-14 (Source 41, page 34) and check for proper static balance, rebalancing as required per Section 18 (Source 12, page 40).

query_text: My magnetic compass has large deviations -- the heading it shows is way off from what it should be.
validated_fix: Swing the compass. A qualified A&P or avionics technician swings the compass by placing the aircraft on known magnetic headings and adjusting the compensating magnets inside the compass to minimize deviation. A new compass deviation card is then created showing residual deviations on all cardinal and intercardinal headings. Maximum allowable residual deviation: ±10° per FAR 23.1327. After swinging, install the new deviation card on or near the compass. NOTE: The compass should be re-swung after any avionics installation (new radios, speakers, intercom), any electrical system changes, or any structural work near the compass that could affect the magnetic field. Also verify the compass fluid is clear and bubble-free -- a cloudy compass or one with a large bubble is unreliable and must be serviced or replaced.

response_parsed.diagnostic_reasoning: Large deviations in the magnetic compass typically indicate a need for compensation. The Service Manual states that 'No maintenance is required on compass except on occasional check on a compass rose for adjustment of compensation' (Source 1, 8, Section 15-50). Furthermore, 'Magnetic compass compensation' is listed as a special inspection item every 200 hours (Source 7). The compass is equipped with compensating magnets adjustable from the front of the case (Source 1, 8). Therefore, the most probable cause is that the compass is out of compensation.
response_parsed.mechanic_instructions: The magnetic compass requires compensation. You need to perform an 'occasional check on a compass rose for adjustment of compensation' as per Section 15-50. The compass has compensating magnets adjustable from the front of the case. Conduct a compass swing and adjust the compensating magnets to correct the deviations.
answer_path_prompts: I performed the compensation, and the compass now reads correctly.
I performed the compensation, but large deviations persist, or I was unable to compensate it accurately.

query_text: The fuel selector is leaking internally -- fuel is cross-feeding between tanks or dripping from the valve.
validated_fix: Overhaul the fuel selector valve -- replace all internal O-rings and seals with fuel-resistant Viton materials. Test for leaks and verify all detent positions. NOTE: Any fuel leak in the cabin is a critical safety issue -- fuel vapors in the cockpit are an explosion hazard.

source_citations.source: 1975_cessna_172_skyhawk_aviation/.splits_Cessna 172 & Skyhawk Series Service Manual (1969 - 1976)/Cessna 172 & Skyhawk Series Service Manual (1969 - 1976)_part01_p1-50.pdf
source_citations.context: 2-44. FUEL SELECTOR VALVE

response_parsed.diagnostic_reasoning: The reported symptom of internal fuel selector leakage, including cross-feeding or dripping from the valve, points directly to a defective fuel selector valve. The troubleshooting guide (Section 12-4) lists 'Defective fuel selector valve' as a probable cause for fuel flow issues. Section 12-13 explicitly states that 'Valve repair consists of replacement of seals, springs, balls and other detail parts,' indicating that internal component failure is a known issue. The 100-hour inspection also calls for checking the valve for cleanliness, wear, and proper operation (Section 2-44). The immediate next step is to remove the valve for a thorough inspection of its internal components.
response_parsed.mechanic_instructions: The internal fuel selector leak, whether cross-feeding or dripping, indicates a failure within the valve itself. This is likely due to worn or damaged internal seals, springs, or balls. To confirm this, you need to remove the fuel selector valve for a detailed inspection. Follow the procedure in Section 12-14, 'REMOVAL AND INSTALLATION,' starting on page 3 of the Fuel System section. Pay close attention to precautions in paragraph 12-3 regarding fuel drainage. Once removed, inspect the valve for physical signs of wear or damage.
answer_path_prompts: Upon disassembly, I found visible wear, scoring, or damage to the internal seals, balls, or valve body.
The valve's internal components appear intact with no obvious damage, but there is evidence of fuel residue or staining indicating a past leak path.
I found no visible damage or fuel residue on the valve itself, but the fuel lines connected to the valve showed signs of leakage or improper sealing.

query_text: The elevator hinge bolts are worn and there's excessive play in the elevator control surface.
validated_fix: Replace worn hinge bolts and bushings. Remove the elevator, inspect all hinge points for elongated bolt holes and worn bushings. Reassemble and verify control surface free play within Cessna limits. Check elevator travel: UP = 28° ± 1°, DOWN = 23° ± 1° (verify for specific model). NOTE: Excessive play can cause flutter -- a destructive oscillation that can destroy the tail in seconds.

response_parsed.diagnostic_reasoning: The user reports worn elevator hinge bolts and excessive play in the elevator control surface. The Service Manual lists 'Defective elevator hinges' as a probable cause for 'BINDING OR JUMPY MOTION FELT IN MOVEMENT OF ELEVATORS' and prescribes 'Replace hinges found defective' (Source 1, page 30; Source 7, page 30; Source 15, page 30). Excessive play is a direct indicator of a defective hinge. Furthermore, the manual specifies that cracks in the front spar at hinge fittings or in the structure supporting the overhanging balance weight are *not* considered negligible damage (Source 2, page 40; Source 12, page 40), highlighting the critical nature of hinge integrity and surrounding structure. Before proceeding with hinge replacement, it is essential to confirm the extent of the hinge damage and inspect for any secondary damage to the surrounding airframe structure. Additionally, other components in the elevator control system, such as bellcranks or cables, could contribute to perceived play or binding, and should be quickly checked.
response_parsed.mechanic_instructions: You've identified worn elevator hinge bolts and excessive play. This points directly to defective elevator hinges. Before replacement, we need to confirm the full extent of the damage and rule out other contributing factors. Carefully inspect the elevator hinges and the surrounding structure for any cracks or additional wear. Also, check the bellcranks and cable tension, as these can also cause play or binding in the control system.
answer_path_prompts: The elevator hinges show significant wear, and there are visible cracks in the front spar at the hinge fittings.
The elevator hinges show wear, but no cracks are visible. However, the bellcrank or its pivot bearing also has noticeable play.
The elevator hinges show wear, but no cracks are visible. The bellcrank is solid, but the elevator control cables are slack.

query_text: My magnetic compass has large deviations -- the heading it shows is way off from what it should be.
validated_fix: Swing the compass per FAR 23.1327 -- maximum allowable residual deviation: ±10°. Create a new deviation card. Re-swing after any avionics or electrical system changes. Verify compass fluid is clear and bubble-free.

response_parsed.diagnostic_reasoning: The magnetic compass is equipped with compensating magnets adjustable from the front of the case to account for magnetic interference within the aircraft. The Service Manual explicitly states that the only required maintenance, beyond lamp replacement, is an occasional check on a compass rose for adjustment of compensation. Large deviations directly point to an issue with the current compensation settings or the need for a new compensation procedure. This is a 200-hour inspection item.
response_parsed.mechanic_instructions: The magnetic compass is designed with internal compensating magnets to correct for magnetic fields within the aircraft that cause deviations. When the compass shows large deviations, the most probable cause is that the compass compensation is incorrect or has not been performed recently. We need to establish the history of the compass compensation.
answer_path_prompts: The compass has not been compensated recently, or the last compensation was performed a long time ago.
The compass was recently compensated, but the deviations are still significant.
The deviations are inconsistent, changing with electrical load or aircraft attitude.

query_text: My altimeter doesn't match field elevation with the current setting -- it's off by over 75 feet.
validated_fix: Have the altimeter inspected per FAR 91.411 (required every 24 months for IFR). Allowable tolerance: ±75 feet at sea level. Overhaul or replace if out of tolerance. NOTE: An inaccurate altimeter is dangerous even for VFR -- can lead to CFIT or airspace violations.

response_parsed.diagnostic_reasoning: An altimeter indicating off by over 75 feet with the correct setting points to an 'INCORRECT INDICATION' as per Section 15-20, TROUBLE SHOOTING--ALTIMETER. The probable causes include a plugged static line, defective altimeter mechanism (e.g., leaking diaphragm, pointers out of calibration), or leaks in the static system, potentially affecting other instruments. The most logical first step is to verify the integrity of the static system, as it affects the altimeter, vertical speed indicator, and airspeed indicator. The FSM provides a specific 'STATIC PRESSURE SYSTEM INSPECTION AND LEAKAGE TEST' in Section 15-14.
response_parsed.mechanic_instructions: An altimeter discrepancy of over 75 feet with the correct setting indicates a fault in the static system or the instrument itself. First, ensure the altimeter setting knob is not simply misaligned. Then, we need to check the static pressure system for leaks or obstructions, as this is a common cause for incorrect altimeter readings and affects all three static instruments. Perform the 'STATIC PRESSURE SYSTEM INSPECTION AND LEAKAGE TEST' as outlined in Section 15-14 of the Service Manual. This involves attaching a suction source to the static pressure opening, slowly applying suction until the altimeter indicates a 1000-foot increase, then cutting off the suction to maintain a closed system for one minute. Observe the altimeter for altitude loss.
answer_path_prompts: The altimeter loses more than 100 feet of altitude in one minute during the leakage test.
The altimeter loses 100 feet of altitude or less in one minute during the leakage test.
The altimeter hands were simply misaligned, and resetting them with the knob corrected the 75-foot discrepancy.

query_text: The mechanic found a crack in the nose gear fork during the 100-hour inspection.
validated_fix: Replace per AD 71-22-02: dye-penetrant inspection every 100 hours or after hard landing/severe shimmy. If aircraft has 1,500+ hours, earlier-type fork must be replaced regardless. NOTE: Nose gear fork failure = nose gear collapse. This is a recurring AD.

response_parsed.diagnostic_reasoning: A crack in a structural component like the nose gear fork is a critical failure. The Service Manual consistently mandates replacement for cracked or defective structural parts, rather than repair. For landing gear, the manual states to 'replace defective parts' (Source 13). For other critical components like wheel halves, it specifies 'Cracked wheel halves shall be discarded and new parts used' (Source 29). Furthermore, a general directive for actuator assemblies states, 'DO NOT attempt to repair damaged or worn parts... Discard all defective items and install new parts during reassembly' (Source 10). The nose gear fork is an integral part of the nose gear shock strut assembly, and its removal is detailed during strut disassembly (Source 8). Therefore, a cracked nose gear fork must be replaced.
response_parsed.mechanic_instructions: A crack in the nose gear fork is a critical defect that necessitates immediate replacement. Do not attempt to repair the fork. You will need to remove the nose gear shock strut assembly to access and replace the fork. Refer to paragraph 5-26 for Nose Gear Removal and Installation, and paragraph 5-33 for Nose Gear Shock Strut Disassembly, which details the removal of the fork from the strut. (Source 8, 17, 23, 33).
answer_path_prompts: I will proceed with the nose gear shock strut removal and disassembly to replace the fork.
I need to confirm the correct part number for the replacement nose gear fork.

query_text: I hear a whining noise in my radios that changes pitch with engine RPM.
validated_fix: Check alternator filter capacitor, all avionics grounds, ignition harness shielding, and voltage regulator. Systematic cleaning of all ground connections usually resolves the issue. Install alternator noise filter if needed.

source_citations.source: 1975_cessna_172_skyhawk_aviation/.splits_Cessna 172 & Skyhawk Series Service Manual (1969 - 1976)/Cessna 172 & Skyhawk Series Service Manual (1969 - 1976)_part06_p251-300.pdf

source_citations.source: 1975_cessna_172_skyhawk_aviation/.splits_Cessna 172 & Skyhawk Series Service Manual (1969 - 1976)/Cessna 172 & Skyhawk Series Service Manual (1969 - 1976)_part06_p251-300.pdf
source_citations.context: Figure 16-5. Voltage Regulator Installation

response_parsed.diagnostic_reasoning: The whining noise in the radios that changes pitch with engine RPM is a classic symptom of electrical interference originating from the charging system. The Service Manual explicitly links 'excessive radio noise' to poor voltage regulation and dirty connections on the alternator, wiring shields, and voltage regulator (Source 12, page 8). It also details troubleshooting for a 'radio noise filter' and associated wiring when a 'shorted radio noise filter or shorted wire' is a probable cause (Source 2, page 3; Source 7, page 3). A faulty alternator or regulator (e.g., shorted diodes) can also generate this noise (Source 21, page 4). The most direct initial check for radio noise is the dedicated radio noise filter and its wiring.
response_parsed.mechanic_instructions: The whining noise indicates electrical interference, likely from the alternator system. We need to check the radio noise filter and its wiring first, as this component is specifically designed to suppress such noise. Disconnect the cable connections from the radio noise filter. Then, check the resistance from the filter input terminal to ground. Next, check the resistance from ground to the free ends of the wires that were connected to the radio noise filter.
answer_path_prompts: The resistance reading from the filter input terminal to ground indicates a direct short (not infinite resistance).
The resistance reading from the filter input terminal to ground is infinite, but the resistance from ground to the free ends of the wires connected to the filter indicates a direct short.
All resistance readings (filter to ground, and wires to ground) show infinite resistance, indicating no direct shorts in the filter or its immediate wiring.

query_text: There's oil dripping from the oil cooler and the level keeps dropping.
validated_fix: Replace or repair the oil cooler. Replace leaking fittings and O-rings. If the core is leaking, replace the entire cooler with an FAA-PMA unit. Also check the vernatherm (bypass valve). NOTE: Oil on the exhaust system is a fire hazard -- repair before further flight.

source_citations.context: 11-39. OIL COOLER.

response_parsed.diagnostic_reasoning: The reported symptom of oil dripping from the oil cooler and a dropping oil level points directly to a leak in the engine oil system, specifically at the oil cooler or its connections. The Service Manual lists 'Oilcooler' as an item to check for leaks during inspection (Source 5, page 38). The oil cooler uses flexible hoses to carry oil to and from the engine (Source 13, page 16; Source 16, page 16), which are common points for leaks. While other engine oil leaks are possible (e.g., crankshaft seal, push rod housing, Source 10, page 14), the direct observation of dripping from the cooler makes the cooler assembly the primary suspect.
response_parsed.mechanic_instructions: You've got oil dripping from the oil cooler and a dropping oil level. This indicates a leak in the oil cooling system. The first step is to pinpoint the exact source of the leak. Clean the area around the oil cooler thoroughly, then run the engine to operating temperature and visually inspect for the leak. Pay close attention to the cooler core, the flexible hoses, and their fittings.
answer_path_prompts: Oil is visibly seeping from the oil cooler core itself.
Oil is dripping from the flexible hoses connected to the oil cooler or their fittings.
The oil cooler and its hoses are dry, but oil is leaking from another area of the engine, such as the front crankshaft seal or push rod housings.

query_text: The starter engages but won't turn the engine -- grinding noise and the starter just spins.
validated_fix: Inspect starter adapter and ring gear. Replace starter adapter clutch (Lycoming LW-16471) if worn. Replace ring gear if teeth are damaged (requires engine removal). NOTE: Continued attempts with a failed adapter further damages the ring gear.

response_parsed.diagnostic_reasoning: The symptom 'starter engages but won't turn the engine -- grinding noise and the starter just spins' indicates a mechanical failure in the engagement between the starter motor and the engine's crankshaft. The Service Manual's 'TROUBLE SHOOTING' section for 'STARTER MOTOR RUNS, BUT DOES NOT TURN CRANKSHAFT' lists 'Defective Bendix drive' and 'Damaged starter pinion gear or ring gear' as probable causes (Source 1, 8, 16, 33, 45). The 'STARTER EXCESSIVELY NOISY' troubleshooting also points to 'Worn starter pinion gear or broken teeth on ring gear' (Source 1, 8, 14, 33). The Bendix drive (or overrunning clutch) is responsible for meshing the starter pinion with the ring gear (Source 11, 20). A failure in either the drive mechanism or the gears themselves would produce the described grinding and spinning.
response_parsed.mechanic_instructions: The grinding noise and the starter spinning without turning the crankshaft points to a failure in the mechanical connection between the starter motor and the engine's ring gear. This is typically caused by either a defective Bendix drive (overrunning clutch) or damaged teeth on the starter pinion gear or the engine's ring gear. You need to remove the starter motor to inspect these components. Disconnect the electrical cable at the starter motor, ensuring the terminal bolt does not rotate to prevent internal damage (Source 24, 26, 44). Then remove the nuts and bolt securing the starter to the crankcase (Source 34). Once the starter is removed, inspect the Bendix drive and the starter pinion gear. Also, visually inspect the engine's ring gear for damage.
answer_path_prompts: The starter pinion gear is worn, chipped, or has broken teeth, or the engine's ring gear shows similar damage.
The Bendix drive (overrunning clutch) is visibly damaged, stuck, or does not extend/retract properly.
Both the starter pinion gear and the engine's ring gear appear normal, and the Bendix drive functions correctly.

headings: 11-13. INSPECTION. For specific items to be in-

query_text: My wheel fairings are cracked and loose -- rattling and pieces coming off.
validated_fix: Repair or replace fairings. Cracked fairings can be repaired with approved fiberglass methods per AC 43.13-1B. Verify all mounting hardware. NOTE: Loose fairings can jam rudder cables or become FOD. Many operators remove fairings for training use -- acceptable, but reduces cruise by ~3-5 knots.

source_citations.source: 1975_cessna_172_skyhawk_aviation/.splits_Cessna 172 & Skyhawk Series Service Manual (1969 - 1976)/Cessna 172 & Skyhawk Series Service Manual (1969 - 1976)_part06_p251-300.pdf
source_citations.context: 18-41. WING LEADING EDGE. > 18-42. NEGLIGIBLE DAMAGE. Refer to paragraph > 18-13.

response_parsed.diagnostic_reasoning: The user reports cracked and loose wheel fairings, with rattling and pieces coming off. This indicates a direct failure of the fairing material or its attachment. The Service Manual lists 'Main gear wheels and fairings' and 'Nose gear wheel, torque links, steering rods, boots and fairing' as items for 200-hour inspection, indicating they are subject to wear and damage (Source 2, 8). Procedures for fairing removal and installation are provided in paragraphs 5-10 and 5-27/5-28 (Source 7, 24, 26, 28, 30, 47, 48, 50). The manual also refers to Cessna Service Kit SK182-12 for repair of wheel speed fairings on specific models (Source 7, 24), implying repairability for some damage. However, 'pieces coming off' suggests damage beyond minor repair. Incorrect scraper-to-tire clearance (0.38 inch nominal) is a critical adjustment that, if incorrect, could lead to fairing stress and damage (Source 7, 14, 22, 24, 28, 36). Finally, underlying landing gear structural issues, such as wrinkles in the main landing gear bulkhead areas, could contribute to fairing stress (Source 10). The initial step is to visually assess the fairing's condition and its mounting.
response_parsed.mechanic_instructions: The wheel fairings are cracked and loose, indicating a potential failure of the fairing material or its mounting. We need to determine the extent of the damage to the fairing itself and check for any contributing factors like improper installation or underlying structural issues. Visually inspect the affected wheel fairing(s) and their attachment points.
answer_path_prompts: The fairing material is cracked, broken, or missing pieces, and/or the mounting screws/nutplates are stripped or damaged, allowing significant movement.
The fairing appears structurally sound, but the scraper is visibly rubbing the tire, or there's a significant gap (more than 0.38 inch) between the scraper and the tire.
The fairing is damaged, but I also observe cracks, deformation, or excessive movement in the main landing gear spring-strut or its attaching bulkhead.

query_text: Corrosion found at wing strut attach fittings during the annual.
validated_fix: Inspect per Cessna criteria. Clean corrosion, treat with conversion coating, protect with primer. If pitting exceeds depth limits, fitting must be replaced per approved data. Clear drain holes. Apply ACF-50 or CorrosionX preventively. NOTE: Strut fittings are critical structural components -- corrosion here requires immediate attention.

response_parsed.diagnostic_reasoning: Corrosion at the wing strut attach fittings is a critical structural concern. The Service Manual identifies the wing strut, wing attachment fitting, and fuselage attachment fitting as key components in this area (Figure 4-2). For specific aircraft serials (17261664 THRU 17261808), wing strut attachment straps require inspection per Service Letter SE73-20. General airframe corrosion inspection is also a monthly requirement. The extent and location of the corrosion will determine if repair or replacement is necessary, as wing strut repair is limited to attaching parts, and a badly dented, cracked, or deformed strut should be replaced. Wing and fuselage attachment fittings are integral to the wing structure.
response_parsed.mechanic_instructions: Corrosion at the wing strut attach fittings demands immediate attention due to its structural implications. We need to determine the exact location and severity of the corrosion. First, identify if the corrosion is on the wing strut itself, the wing attachment fitting, the fuselage attachment fitting, or the attachment straps. Pay close attention to aircraft serials 17261664 THRU 17261808, as these have a specific inspection requirement for wing strut attachment straps per Cessna Single-engine Service Letter SE73-20. Remove the strut fairings as outlined in paragraph 4-10.a to gain full access.
answer_path_prompts: Corrosion is primarily on the wing strut attachment straps, particularly on an aircraft within serials 17261664 THRU 17261808.
Corrosion is primarily on the wing attachment fitting (part 3 in Figure 4-2) or the fuselage attachment fitting (part 8 in Figure 4-2).
Corrosion is primarily on the wing strut tube itself (part 1 in Figure 4-2), causing deformation or cracking.
Corrosion is minor, superficial, and localized to the fairings or non-structural hardware.

query_text: The ELT battery is expired and the unit failed its annual test.
validated_fix: Replace ELT battery per FAR 91.207. Test per regulations (first 5 minutes of any hour, max 3 sweeps). Consider upgrading older 121.5 MHz ELT to 406 MHz with GPS -- 121.5 is no longer satellite-monitored.

source_citations.source: 1975_cessna_172_skyhawk_aviation/.splits_Cessna 172 & Skyhawk Series Service Manual (1969 - 1976)/Cessna 172 & Skyhawk Series Service Manual (1969 - 1976)_part06_p251-300.pdf
source_citations.context: 16-95. TROUBLE SHOOTING

response_parsed.diagnostic_reasoning: The ELT unit failing its annual test, coupled with an expired battery, strongly suggests a 'POWER LOW' condition. The troubleshooting guide for the ELT (Section 16-95) lists 'Low battery voltage' as a probable cause for 'POWER LOW'. The first step in the remedy is to measure the battery-pack voltage. If the voltage is below specification (10.8V for 6-cell magnesium or 11.2V for 4-cell lithium), the battery pack is the confirmed issue. If the battery voltage is within specification, the troubleshooting then directs to checking the transmitter or co-axial antenna cable.
response_parsed.mechanic_instructions: Given the ELT battery is expired and the unit failed its annual test, the most probable cause is low battery voltage. The Service Manual's troubleshooting for 'POWER LOW' (Section 16-95) directs us to verify the battery's output. Set the ELT toggle switch to OFF. Remove the plastic plug from the remote jack and connect a Simpson 260 model voltmeter using a Switchcraft #750 jackplug. Measure the battery-pack voltage.
answer_path_prompts: The 6-cell magnesium battery pack voltage is 10.8 volts or less, OR the 4-cell lithium battery pack voltage is 11.2 volts or less.
The battery pack voltage meets or exceeds the specified minimums (above 10.8V for 6-cell magnesium, above 11.2V for 4-cell lithium).

query_text: My tachometer reads inconsistently -- different from actual RPMs.
validated_fix: Check and calibrate/replace the tachometer. The 172I-M uses a mechanical cable-driven tach. Replace cable if worn/dry. Verify accuracy with hand-held digital tachometer -- max allowable error: ±35 RPM. NOTE: Inaccurate tach affects mixture management and RPM limits.

response_parsed.diagnostic_reasoning: The primary suspect for an inconsistent tachometer reading is the mechanical drive shaft, as the Service Manual states, 'Most tachometer difficulties will be found in the drive-shaft.' (Source 10, 15-34). Issues can include kinks, dents, sharp bends in the housing, or worn spots, breaks, and kinks in the cable itself. Improper seating of the cable in the engine fitting or tachometer is also a probable cause. If the drive system is sound, the instrument itself could be defective, though this is less common for tachometers according to the manual's emphasis.
response_parsed.mechanic_instructions: An inconsistent tachometer reading typically points to an issue with the mechanical drive system. The tachometer is driven at half crankshaft speed by a flexible shaft. We need to physically inspect this drive system. Begin by examining the external condition of the tachometer drive shaft housing.
answer_path_prompts: The drive shaft housing has visible kinks, dents, or sharp bends, or the cable feels rough when pulled out.
The drive shaft housing appears normal, and the cable pulls out smoothly without visible wear or kinks.
The tachometer is also making a noticeable noise or the pointer is oscillating excessively.

query_text: Engine runs rough -- magneto timing has drifted from specification.
validated_fix: Retime magnetos to 20° BTDC (O-320-E2D spec). Use timing light/buzzer. Verify both mags fire at 20° BTDC ± 1°. Timing drifts from point wear, loose mounting, or impulse coupling wear. Check at every annual.

source_citations.source: 1975_cessna_172_skyhawk_aviation/.splits_Cessna 172 & Skyhawk Series Service Manual (1969 - 1976)/Cessna 172 & Skyhawk Series Service Manual (1969 - 1976)_part04_p151-200.pdf
source_citations.context: 11-58. MAINTENANCE. Magneto-to-engine timing should be checked at the first 50 hours, first 100 hours and thereafter at each 200 hours. If timing to the engine is not within plus zero degrees and minus two degrees, the magneto should be retimed to the engine.

source_citations.source: 1975_cessna_172_skyhawk_aviation/.splits_Cessna 172 & Skyhawk Series Service Manual (1969 - 1976)/Cessna 172 & Skyhawk Series Service Manual (1969 - 1976)_part04_p151-200.pdf
source_citations.context: llA-55. MAGNETO-TO-ENGINE TIMING. The magneto must be installed with its timing marks correctly aligned and with the number one cylinder on its compression stroke with number one piston at its advanced firing position.

source_citations.source: 1975_cessna_172_skyhawk_aviation/.splits_Cessna 172 & Skyhawk Series Service Manual (1969 - 1976)/Cessna 172 & Skyhawk Series Service Manual (1969 - 1976)_part04_p151-200.pdf
source_citations.context: A sharp rpm drop-off past normal is usually a sign of a fouled plug, a defective harness lead or a magneto out of time.

response_parsed.diagnostic_reasoning: The user reports a rough running engine and suspects magneto timing drift. The Service Manual confirms that magneto-to-engine timing should be checked at regular intervals (50, 100, then 200 hours for Lycoming; 100 hours for Continental) and retimed if outside the +0/-2 degree tolerance (Source 1, 9, 11). A magneto out of time can cause a sharp RPM drop-off during a magneto check, indicating a rough running condition (Source 38). The immediate next step is to verify the magneto-to-engine timing for both magnetos.
response_parsed.mechanic_instructions: You've identified magneto timing drift as the probable cause for the rough running engine. We need to verify the magneto-to-engine timing. This procedure involves setting the engine to the advanced firing position for cylinder number one and then using a timing light to check the magneto's contact break point. Refer to paragraph 11-57 (Lycoming) or 11A-55 (Continental) for the detailed procedure on locating the advanced firing position and timing the magneto to the engine (Source 18, 20, 25, 35, 42, 50). Pay close attention to the final motion of the crankshaft being in the normal direction of rotation when aligning timing marks. Once the engine is set, connect a timing light to the capacitor terminal at the rear of the magneto and to a good ground (Source 34, 47). Slowly advance the propeller in the normal direction of rotation until the timing light indicates the contacts break (Source 34, 44). Note the position. Repeat for the other magneto. What do you observe?
answer_path_prompts: The timing for one or both magnetos is outside the +0/-2 degree tolerance, confirming the drift.
The timing for both magnetos is within the +0/-2 degree tolerance, indicating the drift is not the primary issue.
The timing light does not flash, or the spark is inconsistent, suggesting a magneto internal issue or wiring problem.
I encountered difficulty locating the advanced firing position or aligning the timing marks.

query_text: The trim wheel is stiff -- trim doesn't respond smoothly.
validated_fix: Inspect and service the trim system. Replace cable if frayed/corroded. Inspect pulleys for wear. Lubricate the jackscrew mechanism. Verify full travel and correct sense after any work. NOTE: Inoperative trim affects controllability -- especially fatiguing on long flights.

response_parsed.diagnostic_reasoning: The symptom 'trim wheel is stiff -- trim doesn't respond smoothly' directly corresponds to 'TRIM CONTROL WHEEL MOVES WITH EXCESSIVE RESISTANCE' in the Elevator Trim Control System troubleshooting section. Probable causes include high cable tension, binding pulleys, rusty or binding chains/sprockets, bent sprocket shafts, binding trim tab hinges, or a defective trim tab actuator. The initial diagnostic steps should aim to isolate the source of this resistance.
response_parsed.mechanic_instructions: The trim control wheel's excessive resistance indicates a mechanical binding or improper tension within the elevator trim control system. We need to systematically check the components from the control wheel to the trim tab. Begin by checking the cable tension and visually inspecting the accessible mechanical components.
answer_path_prompts: The trim control cables are excessively tight when checked per Figure 9-1 specifications.
Upon opening access plates, I observe rusty chains, damaged sprockets, or cables not properly seated on pulleys.
After disconnecting the actuator chain, the trim tab hinge moves freely, but the actuator itself is stiff when operated manually.
After disconnecting the actuator chain, the trim tab hinge is stiff and resists movement.

query_text: The carb heat knob is very stiff to pull -- doesn't give full heat.
technical_issue: Carburetor Heat Control Cable Stiff / Broken
validated_fix: Replace or lubricate the carb heat cable. Verify full travel -- valve must open/close completely. CRITICAL: Inoperative carb heat is a GROUNDING ITEM. Carburetor ice can cause complete engine failure and carb heat is the only defense.

source_citations.source: 1975_cessna_172_skyhawk_aviation/.splits_Cessna 172 & Skyhawk Series Service Manual (1969 - 1976)/Cessna 172 & Skyhawk Series Service Manual (1969 - 1976)_part04_p151-200.pdf
source_citations.context: 11A- 46. AIRBOX.

response_parsed.diagnostic_reasoning: The symptom of a stiff carburetor heat knob that doesn't provide full heat points to a mechanical issue within the carburetor heat control system. The primary suspects are binding in the control cable or its housing, a sticking carburetor heat valve in the airbox, or improper rigging of the control. The Service Manual states that 'Most of the operational troubles in the heating... systems are caused by sticking or binding air valves and/or their controls, damaged air ducting, or defects in the exhaust muffler.' (Source 2, 8). The carburetor heat control operates a valve in the induction airbox (Source 56, 58, 59). Rigging procedures for the carburetor heat control emphasize smooth travel and full range of motion (Source 13, 21). We need to isolate whether the binding is in the control cable itself or the valve it operates.
response_parsed.mechanic_instructions: The stiff carburetor heat knob and lack of full heat indicate a mechanical issue. We need to determine if the binding is in the control cable, the airbox valve, or if the control is simply mis-rigged. Remove the engine cowling per paragraph 11-3. Disconnect the carburetor heat control wire from the arm on the airbox (Source 50, 58). Then, physically test the movement of both the control wire and the airbox arm.
answer_path_prompts: With the engine cowling removed, I disconnected the carburetor heat control wire from the arm on the airbox. The control wire itself is stiff to pull and push from the cockpit.

With the engine cowling removed, I disconnected the carburetor heat control wire from the arm on the airbox. The control wire moves freely, but the arm on the airbox (which operates the valve) is stiff or stuck.

With the engine cowling removed, I disconnected the carburetor heat control wire from the arm on the airbox. Both the control wire and the airbox arm move freely, but when reconnected and the knob is pulled full out, the airbox arm does not reach its full open position.

query_text: Oil weeping from the back of the engine around magneto/accessory area.
validated_fix: Replace accessory case gaskets and magneto mount pad gaskets. Torque all fasteners per Lycoming manual. Check mounting studs for looseness. NOTE: Oil on exhaust = fire hazard. Progressive oil loss can reach dangerously low levels.

source_citations.source: 1975_cessna_172_skyhawk_aviation/.splits_Cessna 172 & Skyhawk Series Service Manual (1969 - 1976)/Cessna 172 & Skyhawk Series Service Manual (1969 - 1976)_part04_p151-200.pdf
source_citations.context: 11-31. TROUBLE SHOOTING. Oil pressure line broken, disconnected or pinched.

response_parsed.diagnostic_reasoning: Oil weeping from the magneto/accessory area suggests a breach in the engine's full-pressure, wet sump lubricating system (Source 10, 18). The most probable causes are compromised sealing surfaces or damaged lines in this vicinity. This includes the magneto mounting gaskets (Source 40), accessory housing gaskets, or flexible fluid hoses and fittings (Source 12, 45). A defective pressure relief valve, located at the aft end of the right oil gallery, could also be a source of weeping (Source 32). The initial step is to clean the area and observe the exact origin of the leak to narrow down the component.
response_parsed.mechanic_instructions: Clean the engine compartment thoroughly, especially the area around the magnetos and accessory housing, to remove all oil residue. This will allow for a clear visual identification of the leak's origin. Once clean, run the engine at idle for a short period and carefully observe where the oil begins to weep from.
answer_path_prompts: Oil is weeping directly from around the base of one of the magnetos, indicating a magneto gasket or seal issue.
Oil is weeping from a seam or gasket on the main accessory housing, not directly from a magneto.
Oil is weeping from an oil line or fitting connected to the accessory housing or a magneto.
Oil is weeping from the pressure relief valve at the aft end of the right oil gallery.
The source of the leak is unclear, or it appears to be coming from a different area of the engine.

answer_paths: Oil is weeping directly from around the base of one of the magnetos, indicating a magneto gasket or seal issue.
Oil is weeping from a seam or gasket on the main accessory housing, not directly from a magneto.
Oil is weeping from an oil line or fitting connected to the accessory housing or a magneto.
Oil is weeping from the pressure relief valve at the aft end of the right oil gallery.
The source of the leak is unclear, or it appears to be coming from a different area of the engine.
mechanic_instructions: Clean the engine compartment thoroughly, especially the area around the magnetos and accessory housing, to remove all oil residue. This will allow for a clear visual identification of the leak's origin. Once clean, run the engine at idle for a short period and carefully observe where the oil begins to weep from.
diagnostic_reasoning: Oil weeping from the magneto/accessory area suggests a breach in the engine's full-pressure, wet sump lubricating system (Source 10, 18). The most probable causes are compromised sealing surfaces or damaged lines in this vicinity. This includes the magneto mounting gaskets (Source 40), accessory housing gaskets, or flexible fluid hoses and fittings (Source 12, 45). A defective pressure relief valve, located at the aft end of the right oil gallery, could also be a source of weeping (Source 32). The initial step is to clean the area and observe the exact origin of the leak to narrow down the component.

query_text: The engine has developed a persistent miss and the mechanic says a cam lobe is worn flat.
validated_fix: Replace camshaft and all lifters. Use ZDDP-rich assembly lube and break in at 2,000+ RPM for 20 minutes. Use oil with adequate ZDDP content (Aeroshell W100 Plus or equivalent). NOTE: Flat-tappet cam wear is accelerated by modern low-ZDDP oils. Always use aviation-specific oil.

response_parsed.diagnostic_reasoning: A persistent miss and a worn flat cam lobe point directly to a mechanical issue within the engine, specifically affecting valve lift and thus cylinder compression. The Service Manual lists 'Defective engine' and 'Perform an engine compression check' as a remedy for 'ENGINE RUNS ROUGHLY OR WILL NOT ACCELERATE PROPERLY' (Source 14, 18, 34). A worn cam lobe would cause low compression on the affected cylinder. Other common causes for rough running include ignition system issues (spark plugs, magneto timing, ignition harness), fuel system problems (carburetor, fuel contamination, primer leaks), and induction system issues (air filter, air leaks, carburetor heat control) (Source 9, 14, 18, 31, 34, 35, 43, 44). The FSM also mentions cam lobe lubrication, indicating wear is a consideration (Source 1, 36). The initial steps should verify the mechanical integrity of the engine via a compression check, then move to ignition and fuel/induction systems if compression is good.
response_parsed.mechanic_instructions: A persistent miss, especially with a suspected worn cam lobe, indicates a potential mechanical issue affecting cylinder performance. Your first step is to verify the engine's mechanical integrity. Perform an engine compression check on all cylinders. This will confirm if a specific cylinder is underperforming due to a mechanical fault like a worn cam lobe, which would result in low compression. Also, conduct a magneto drop check during engine run-up to isolate potential ignition system issues, and visually inspect the induction system.
answer_path_prompts: One or more cylinders show significantly low compression (e.g., below manufacturer's minimum or significantly lower than other cylinders).
All cylinders show good, even compression, but there is an excessive magneto drop on one or both magnetos during the run-up.
All cylinders show good, even compression, and magneto drop is normal, but there is a noticeable induction air leak or the carburetor heat control is partially open.

query_text: Fuel gauges read full when tanks are half empty -- can't trust them.
validated_fix: Calibrate or replace fuel quantity senders. Check sender resistance, gauge response, and ground connections. CRITICAL NOTE: Per FAR 23.1337, gauges are only required accurate at EMPTY. NEVER rely solely on gauges -- use time-based fuel calculations as PRIMARY method. Running out of fuel is 100% preventable.

source_citations.context: 12-4. TROUBLE SHOOTING. > TROUBLE > NO FUEL QUANTITY INDICATION.

response_parsed.diagnostic_reasoning: The symptom of fuel gauges reading full when tanks are half empty indicates an incorrect resistance signal being sent to or interpreted by the fuel quantity indicator. The fuel quantity system operates with a float-operated variable-resistance transmitter in each tank, where full fuel produces minimum resistance and maximum current flow, resulting in maximum pointer deflection (full). As fuel level lowers, resistance increases, current decreases, and pointer deflection is smaller (empty). Therefore, a 'full' reading when the tank is half empty suggests the system is seeing lower resistance than it should for the actual fuel level. This could be caused by a defective fuel quantity transmitter, a wiring issue (such as a short to ground or loose connection), or a defective fuel quantity indicator (gauge). The Service Manual lists 'Defective gage or sensing unit' for 'OBVIOUSLY INCORRECT TEMPERATURE READING' (which applies to any gauge), 'Loose or broken lead' for 'EXCESSIVE POINTER OSCILLATION', and 'Defective indicator or transmitter' or 'Loose or broken wiring on indicator or transmitter' for 'ERRATIC READINGS'. It also mentions 'Open circuit' or 'Loose connections or open circuit' for 'NO FUEL QUANTITY INDICATION', which could manifest as an incorrect reading if the circuit integrity is compromised. Transmitter calibration issues are also possible if the float arm or stops are bent. The initial steps should focus on isolating the faulty component by checking the wiring, then the transmitter, and finally the indicator.
response_parsed.mechanic_instructions: The fuel quantity system relies on a variable-resistance transmitter in each wing tank. A 'full' reading when the tank is half empty means the gauge is receiving a signal indicating lower resistance than the actual fuel level. This could be due to a fault in the transmitter, the wiring, or the gauge itself. We need to isolate the component causing this incorrect reading. Start by checking the most accessible points.
answer_path_prompts: I found loose or corroded electrical connections at the fuel quantity transmitter or along the wiring leading to the gauge.

After draining the fuel and accessing the transmitter, manually moving the float arm from 'Empty' to 'Full' does not produce a corresponding change on the fuel gauge, or the gauge remains stuck at 'Full'. (Observe all fuel system precautions, Section 12, page 12-1).
I have substituted the fuel quantity indicator (gauge) with a known-good unit, and the new gauge now reads correctly for the fuel level.

query_text: Visible wobble at the front of the engine -- drive belt keeps coming off.
validated_fix: Replace the harmonic balancer. Remove with proper puller (never hammer). Install new balancer with correct tool. Replace front crank seal at the same time. Verify timing marks on new balancer match old. NOTE: A slipped balancer shows incorrect timing with a timing light.
